question: Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional requirements with examples.
expectedPoints: Functional requirements define what the system must do - specific functions and features
Non-functional requirements define how the system must perform - quality attributes
Example functional: User login, report generation
Example non-functional: 99.9% availability, response time <2 seconds

question: Explain the concept of Throughput and Response Time with suitable examples.
expectedPoints: Throughput: number of transactions/requests processed per unit time
Example: 1000 TPS (transactions per second)
Response Time: total time to complete a single transaction
Example: 500ms response time for a web page load

question: What is a Baseline Measurement? Explain its importance in capacity planning.
expectedPoints: Baseline measurement is a reference set of normal operating performance metrics
Collected over a period to establish normal system behavior
Used to identify anomalies, trends, and plan future capacity
Helps distinguish between normal and abnormal performance

question: List and explain any three key measurements used in IT capacity planning.
expectedPoints: CPU Utilization: percentage of CPU capacity being used
Memory Utilization: percentage of RAM in use
Network Bandwidth: data transfer rate measured in Mbps/Gbps
Disk I/O: read/write operations per second
Throughput: transactions/processes per time unit

question: Explain Workload Characterization and its significance in capacity planning.
expectedPoints: Workload characterization is the process of identifying and categorizing different types of work
Includes transactions, batch jobs, user sessions, queries
Helps understand resource demands of different workloads
Enables accurate capacity forecasting and resource allocation

question: What is the difference between Vertical and Horizontal Scalability?
expectedPoints: Vertical Scaling (Scale-up): Adding more resources to existing server (CPU, RAM, storage)
Horizontal Scaling (Scale-out): Adding more servers/nodes to distribute workload
Vertical has limits; Horizontal offers more scalability
Vertical is simpler; Horizontal requires load balancing and distributed architecture

question: Explain the relationship between Business Requirements and Technical Requirements.
expectedPoints: Business requirements are high-level organizational goals and objectives
Technical requirements are specific technological specifications derived from business needs
Business requirements define WHAT the organization needs to achieve
Technical requirements define HOW to implement those needs in technology

question: Define capacity planning in IT context. Discuss the different kinds of requirements that need to be considered and explain how measurements help in effective capacity management.
expectedPoints: Definition: Process of determining IT resources needed to meet current and future demands
Types of Requirements: Functional, Non-Functional, Business, Technical
Functional: What system must do (features, functions)
Non-Functional: How system must perform (performance, scalability, availability)
Business: High-level organizational goals driving decisions
Technical: Specific hardware/software specifications
Measurements: CPU, Memory, Network, Storage utilization metrics
Throughput, Response Time, Latency as performance indicators
Baselines and KPIs help monitor and plan capacity
Effective capacity management uses measurements to forecast needs and optimize resources

question: Explain Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and Service Level Agreements (SLAs) in capacity planning. How are they related?
expectedPoints: KPI: Measurable values showing effectiveness in achieving business objectives
Examples: Uptime percentage, Average response time, Throughput rate, Error rate
SLA: Contract defining expected service performance metrics
SLA includes: Availability targets, Response time limits, Throughput guarantees
Relationship: SLAs define the targets, KPIs measure whether targets are met
KPIs are used to monitor SLA compliance
Organizations set SLAs based on business needs, then use KPIs to track performance against SLAs

question: Discuss the various metrics and measurements used in IT capacity planning. How do these help in predicting future capacity requirements?
expectedPoints: Resource Metrics: CPU utilization, Memory utilization, Storage capacity, Network bandwidth
Performance Metrics: Throughput, Response time, Latency, I/O operations
Availability Metrics: Uptime percentage, Mean time between failures, Downtime duration
Workload Metrics: Concurrent users, Transaction volumes, Peak load patterns
Measurements help by: Establishing baselines of normal operation
Identifying trends in resource consumption over time
Detecting bottlenecks and performance issues
Enabling trend analysis and forecasting future needs
Supporting what-if scenarios for capacity planning
Validating capacity plans and optimization efforts

question: Explain the concept of scalability in capacity planning. Differentiate between scalability requirements for different types of applications.
expectedPoints: Scalability: System's ability to handle increased workload by adding resources
Vertical Scalability: Adding more power to existing machine (CPU, RAM)
Horizontal Scalability: Adding more machines to handle load
Scalability Requirements vary by application type:
Web Applications: Handle concurrent users, request throughput
Database Applications: Support growing data volumes, query performance
Transaction Processing: Handle peak transaction volumes
Batch Processing: Scale to complete jobs within time windows
Real-time Systems: Maintain response times under increased load
Cloud-native apps: Designed for automatic horizontal scaling
